question: [File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker trigger a take() call where ratifierData encodes a proof array of length 21 or more, causing HashLib.offerTreeTypeHash(proof.length) to revert with TreeTooHigh inside isRatified, permanently blocking any fill of a valid maker offer whose Merkle tree has height > 20, violating the invariant that valid ratified offers must be fillable? Preconditions: maker creates and signs a Merkle tree of height 21; offer is otherwise valid (correct tick, within expiry, maker authorized ratifier). Call sequence: maker signs root of 21-level tree; taker calls Midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, ...) with proof.length == 21; isRatified decodes proof, calls HashLib.isLeaf which calls offerTreeTypeHash(21), which reverts TreeTooHigh; take reverts with RatifierFail. Invariant tested: every valid ratified offer must be fillable by a taker; ratifier must not revert on valid inputs. Scoped impact: fund freeze — maker's entire offer tree is permanently unfillable. Proof idea: unit test with proof.length = 21 and assert take reverts; fuzz proof.length in [21, 256] and assert all revert with TreeTooHigh propagated as RatifierFail.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker supply a ratifierData where leafIndex has bits set above proof.length (e.g., leafIndex = 2**proof.length), causing HashLib.isLeaf to revert with LeafIndexOutOfRange inside isRatified, blocking a valid take even when the offer and proof are otherwise correct? Preconditions: maker has a valid signed Merkle tree; taker constructs ratifierData with leafIndex = 2**proof.length instead of the correct index. Call sequence: taker calls Midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, ...) with malformed leafIndex; isRatified decodes, calls HashLib.isLeaf(root, leafHash, leafIndex, proof); isLeaf checks leafIndex >> proof.length == 0, which fails; reverts LeafIndexOutOfRange; take reverts. Invariant tested: isRatified must not revert on valid offer with valid proof but wrong leafIndex encoding — the error should be InvalidProof not a panic. Scoped impact: fund freeze — taker cannot fill valid offer if they miscalculate leafIndex; also a DoS vector if the error type is unexpected. Proof idea: unit test with leafIndex = 2**proof.length and assert revert is LeafIndexOutOfRange (not InvalidProof); check that integrators expecting only InvalidProof would mishandle this.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker pass ratifierData that is ABI-encoded with a different type layout (e.g., missing the bytes32[] proof field or using wrong types), causing abi.decode(ratifierData, (Signature, bytes32, uint256, bytes32[])) to revert with a generic panic rather than a named error, blocking the take without a meaningful revert reason? Preconditions: taker constructs ratifierData as abi.encode(sig, root, leafIndex) omitting the proof array. Call sequence: taker calls Midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, ...); isRatified calls abi.decode which reverts with a generic revert; take reverts with no named error. Invariant tested: isRatified should revert with a named error for all invalid ratifierData inputs; generic reverts make debugging and integration harder. Scoped impact: fund freeze — taker cannot fill offer if ratifierData is malformed; no clear error for integrators. Proof idea: fuzz ratifierData with truncated, empty, and wrong-type encodings; assert all reverts are named errors from IEcrecoverRatifier.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: cancelRoot / isRatified] Can an authorized operator (authorized by maker via Midnight.setIsAuthorized) call cancelRoot(maker, root) on behalf of the maker without the maker's knowledge, permanently blocking all offers in that Merkle tree, since isRootCanceled is irreversible and there is no uncancelRoot function? Preconditions: maker has authorized operator for unrelated purposes (e.g., to repay debt); operator calls cancelRoot(maker, root) where root covers all of maker's active offers. Call sequence: maker calls Midnight.setIsAuthorized(operator, true, maker); operator calls EcrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot(maker, root); isRootCanceled[maker][root] = true; any subsequent take with that root reverts RootCanceled. Invariant tested: authorization must only allow intended account delegation; coarse authorization must not enable permanent DoS of maker's offer set. Scoped impact: fund freeze — all offers in the canceled tree are permanently unfillable; maker must re-sign and redistribute a new tree. Proof idea: unit test where operator cancels maker's root and assert all subsequent takes revert RootCanceled; verify no uncancelRoot path exists.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: isRatified] Can a maker's authorized delegate signer have their authorization revoked by the maker (or by another authorized operator) between the time offers are signed and the time a taker attempts to fill them, causing isRatified to revert Unauthorized and permanently blocking all offers signed by that delegate? Preconditions: maker authorizes delegate signer; delegate signs a large Merkle tree of offers; maker revokes delegate's authorization; taker attempts to fill any offer from that tree. Call sequence: maker calls Midnight.setIsAuthorized(delegate, true, maker); delegate signs root; maker calls Midnight.setIsAuthorized(delegate, false, maker); taker calls Midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, ...); isRatified calls IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).isAuthorized(offer.maker, _signer) which returns false; reverts Unauthorized. Invariant tested: valid signed offers must remain fillable unless explicitly canceled by the maker; authorization revocation must not silently invalidate all signed offers without maker intent. Scoped impact: fund freeze — all offers signed by the revoked delegate are permanently blocked without explicit cancellation. Proof idea: unit test matching testIsRatifiedRevokeAuthorizationInvalidates; fuzz with multiple delegates and revocation orderings.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: isRatified] Can a taker supply a Signature with v not in {27, 28} (e.g., v = 0, v = 1, v = 26, v = 29) causing ecrecover to return address(0), triggering InvalidSignature revert, even if the offer and Merkle proof are otherwise valid, blocking a take that a correctly-implemented client would have submitted with v = 27 or 28? Preconditions: maker has a valid signed offer; taker or middleware normalizes v incorrectly (e.g., v = 0 from raw secp256k1 output). Call sequence: taker calls Midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, ...) with sig.v = 0; isRatified calls ecrecover(digest, 0, r, s) which returns address(0); require(_signer != address(0), InvalidSignature()) reverts; take reverts RatifierFail. Invariant tested: isRatified must accept all valid EIP-712 signatures; v normalization must be handled or documented. Scoped impact: fund freeze — taker cannot fill valid offer if v is not normalized to {27, 28}. Proof idea: fuzz sig.v over [0, 255] and assert only v in {27, 28} with correct r, s succeeds; assert v = 0 and v = 1 revert InvalidSignature.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: isRatified] Can a taker exploit EIP-2098 compact signature format (where v is encoded in the high bit of s) by passing a Signature struct with v = 27 and s having the high bit set, causing ecrecover to recover a different address than intended, either blocking a valid take (Unauthorized) or accepting an invalid signature? Preconditions: maker signs an offer; taker constructs a compact-format signature and passes it as a standard Signature struct. Call sequence: taker calls Midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, ...) with sig constructed from compact format; isRatified calls ecrecover(digest, sig.v, sig.r, sig.s); recovered address differs from maker or delegate; reverts Unauthorized or InvalidSignature. Invariant tested: isRatified must correctly handle all valid secp256k1 signature encodings; compact format must not cause false positives or false negatives. Scoped impact: fund freeze — valid offers blocked if signature format is misinterpreted. Proof idea: differential test between standard and compact signature encoding for same key and digest.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: isRatified] Can a taker exploit secp256k1 signature malleability (high-s form: s' = n - s, v' = v ^ 1) to produce a second valid signature for the same offer digest, and does isRatified accept both, potentially allowing a taker to fill the same offer with two different ratifierData blobs that both pass signature verification? Preconditions: maker signs an offer with standard (low-s) signature; taker computes the high-s malleable counterpart. Call sequence: taker calls Midnight.take(offer, ratifierData_lowS, ...) — succeeds; taker calls Midnight.take(offer, ratifierData_highS, ...) — also succeeds if ecrecover accepts high-s. Invariant tested: offer fill amount cannot exceed remaining offer capacity; signature malleability must not enable double-fill or replay. Scoped impact: offer bug — maker's offer could be filled twice using two valid signatures for the same digest if consumed tracking is per-group not per-signature. Proof idea: compute malleable signature and assert both ratifierData variants pass isRatified; check if consumed[maker][group] is correctly incremented on both fills.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: isRatified] Can a taker provide a ratifierData where proof.length differs from the proof length used when the maker signed the root (e.g., proof.length = 2 instead of 1), causing offerTreeTypeHash(proof.length) to return a different typehash, making the digest different from what the maker signed, and causing ecrecover to recover a wrong address and revert Unauthorized, blocking a valid take? Preconditions: maker signs root with proof.length = 1 (height-1 tree); taker constructs ratifierData with proof.length = 2 (padding with zero sibling). Call sequence: taker calls Midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, ...) with proof.length = 2; isRatified computes offerTreeTypeHash(2) != offerTreeTypeHash(1); digest differs from what maker signed; ecrecover returns wrong address; reverts Unauthorized. Invariant tested: isRatified must accept exactly the proof length used at signing time; proof length is part of the signed commitment. Scoped impact: fund freeze — taker cannot fill valid offer if proof length is padded or truncated. Proof idea: unit test with proof.length mismatch and assert Unauthorized revert; fuzz proof.length around the correct value.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: isRatified] Can a taker call Midnight.take() with a valid offer and ratifierData where the Merkle proof is correct but the leafIndex is wrong (e.g., leafIndex = 0 for a right-leaf offer), causing isLeaf to compute the wrong root and revert InvalidProof, blocking a valid take due to incorrect leafIndex in the ratifierData? Preconditions: maker has a 2-leaf tree; offer is the right leaf (leafIndex = 1); taker constructs ratifierData with leafIndex = 0. Call sequence: taker calls Midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, ...) with leafIndex = 0; isRatified calls HashLib.isLeaf(root, leafHash, 0, proof); computes wrong root; returns false; reverts InvalidProof. Invariant tested: isRatified must accept the correct leafIndex; wrong leafIndex must revert InvalidProof not a more severe error. Scoped impact: fund freeze — taker cannot fill valid offer if leafIndex is wrong; integrators must correctly track leafIndex per offer. Proof idea: unit test with swapped leafIndex and assert InvalidProof; fuzz leafIndex over [0, 2**proof.length - 1].

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: isRatified] Can a taker call Midnight.take() with a valid offer where the offer's Merkle proof is correct but the root in ratifierData has been canceled by the maker (or an authorized operator) in the same block via a front-run or multicall, causing isRatified to revert RootCanceled and blocking the take even though the offer was valid at the time the taker submitted the transaction? Preconditions: maker has a valid signed tree; taker submits take; maker (or operator) front-runs with cancelRoot in the same block. Call sequence: maker calls EcrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot(maker, root) in same block; taker's Midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, ...) executes after; isRatified checks isRootCanceled[offer.maker][root] == true; reverts RootCanceled. Invariant tested: valid offers must be fillable unless explicitly canceled before the fill; front-running cancellation must be a documented risk. Scoped impact: fund freeze — taker's valid take is blocked by same-block cancellation; taker loses gas. Proof idea: unit test with cancelRoot before take in same block; assert RootCanceled revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: cancelRoot] Can an authorized operator (authorized by maker for unrelated purposes) call cancelRoot(maker, root) to permanently cancel a root that covers partially-filled offers, blocking all remaining fills in that tree even though the maker intended the offers to remain active, since isRootCanceled is irreversible? Preconditions: maker has a large Merkle tree with many offers; some offers have been partially filled; maker has authorized an operator for debt repayment; operator calls cancelRoot. Call sequence: taker partially fills some offers; operator calls EcrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot(maker, root); isRootCanceled[maker][root] = true; all remaining offers in tree are permanently blocked. Invariant tested: authorization must only allow intended account delegation; coarse authorization must not enable permanent cancellation of active offer trees. Scoped impact: fund freeze — remaining offer capacity is permanently lost; maker cannot re-use the same root. Proof idea: unit test where operator cancels root after partial fill; assert remaining fills revert RootCanceled; verify no recovery path.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: isRatified] Can a taker call Midnight.take() with a valid offer where the EIP-712 domain separator in isRatified uses block.chainid (live chain ID) while Midnight uses INITIAL_CHAIN_ID for market IDs, causing a mismatch after a hard fork where block.chainid changes: existing market IDs remain valid but all EcrecoverRatifier signatures become invalid, permanently blocking all offers in all markets that use EcrecoverRatifier? Preconditions: hard fork changes block.chainid; existing markets have valid IDs based on INITIAL_CHAIN_ID; makers have signed offers with old block.chainid in domain separator. Call sequence: after hard fork, taker calls Midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, ...); isRatified computes domainSeparator with new block.chainid; digest differs from what maker signed; ecrecover returns wrong address; reverts Unauthorized. Invariant tested: ratifier must remain consistent with Midnight's chain ID handling; domain separator must not diverge from market ID computation after a fork. Scoped impact: fund freeze — all EcrecoverRatifier-based offers become permanently invalid after a hard fork while markets remain accessible. Proof idea: unit test simulating chainid change (vm.chainId) and assert all existing signatures fail; compare with INITIAL_CHAIN_ID behavior.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: isRatified] Can a taker call Midnight.take() with a valid offer where the offer's callbackData field is very large (e.g., 100KB), causing HashLib.hashOffer to compute keccak256(offer.callbackData) over a large buffer, consuming excessive gas and causing the take to revert due to out-of-gas, blocking a valid take? Preconditions: maker creates an offer with large callbackData; offer is otherwise valid. Call sequence: taker calls Midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, ...) with large callbackData; isRatified calls HashLib.hashOffer(offer) which calls keccak256(offer.callbackData) over large buffer; gas exhaustion causes revert. Invariant tested: isRatified must not be DoS-able via large callbackData; gas cost must be bounded. Scoped impact: fund freeze — taker cannot fill valid offer if callbackData is large; low-cost DoS if maker can force large callbackData. Proof idea: fuzz callbackData.length from 0 to 100KB and measure gas; assert take succeeds within block gas limit.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: isRatified] Can a taker call Midnight.take() with a valid offer where the offer's market.collateralParams array is very large (e.g., 128 entries), causing HashLib.hashMarket to iterate over all entries and compute keccak256 for each, consuming excessive gas and causing the take to revert due to out-of-gas, blocking a valid take? Preconditions: maker creates an offer for a market with many collateral params (up to MAX_COLLATERALS); offer is otherwise valid. Call sequence: taker calls Midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, ...) with large collateralParams; isRatified calls HashLib.hashOffer -> HashLib.hashMarket which iterates over all collateralParams; gas exhaustion causes revert. Invariant tested: isRatified must not be DoS-able via large collateralParams; gas cost must be bounded by MAX_COLLATERALS. Scoped impact: fund freeze — taker cannot fill valid offer if market has many collateral params. Proof idea: fuzz collateralParams.length from 1 to MAX_COLLATERALS and measure gas; assert take succeeds within block gas limit.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: isRatified] Can a taker call Midnight.take() with a valid offer where the offer's market struct in ratifierData differs from the market struct in the offer passed by Midnight (e.g., different collateralParams ordering), causing HashLib.hashOffer to compute a different hash than what the maker signed, and causing isLeaf to return false and revert InvalidProof, blocking a valid take? Preconditions: maker signs offer with collateralParams in sorted order; taker passes offer with different collateralParams ordering to Midnight.take; Midnight passes the offer as-is to isRatified. Call sequence: taker calls Midnight.take(offer_unsorted, ratifierData, ...); isRatified calls HashLib.hashOffer(offer_unsorted) which hashes differently; isLeaf returns false; reverts InvalidProof. Invariant tested: isRatified must accept exactly the offer as signed; offer struct must be passed unchanged from taker to ratifier. Scoped impact: fund freeze — taker cannot fill valid offer if they modify any offer field. Proof idea: unit test with modified offer field and assert InvalidProof; verify Midnight passes offer unchanged to isRatified.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: isRatified] Can a taker call Midnight.take() with a valid offer where the ratifierData contains a valid Merkle proof for a different offer in the same tree (wrong leaf), causing isLeaf to return false and revert InvalidProof, blocking the take even though the taker has a valid proof for a different offer in the same signed tree? Preconditions: maker has a 4-leaf tree; taker has proof for offer at leafIndex 0 but tries to fill offer at leafIndex 1 using the wrong proof. Call sequence: taker calls Midnight.take(offer1, ratifierData_for_offer0, ...); isRatified calls HashLib.isLeaf(root, hashOffer(offer1), 0, proof_for_offer0); computes wrong root; returns false; reverts InvalidProof. Invariant tested: isRatified must reject proofs for wrong offers; each offer must use its own proof and leafIndex. Scoped impact: fund freeze — taker cannot fill valid offer if they use wrong proof; integrators must correctly match proof to offer. Proof idea: unit test with mismatched proof and offer; assert InvalidProof revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: isRatified] Can a taker call Midnight.take() with a valid offer where the Merkle proof contains an extra sibling (proof.length = correct + 1), causing offerTreeTypeHash to use a different typehash than what the maker signed, making the digest different and causing ecrecover to recover a wrong address, reverting Unauthorized and blocking the take? Preconditions: maker signs root with proof.length = 2; taker constructs ratifierData with proof.length = 3 (adding a zero sibling). Call sequence: taker calls Midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, ...) with proof.length = 3; isRatified computes offerTreeTypeHash(3) != offerTreeTypeHash(2); digest differs; ecrecover returns wrong address; reverts Unauthorized. Invariant tested: proof length must exactly match the signed tree height; padding proof with extra siblings must not bypass or block verification. Scoped impact: fund freeze — taker cannot fill valid offer if proof is padded. Proof idea: unit test with padded proof and assert Unauthorized revert; verify that padding cannot be used to bypass signature check.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: isRatified] Can a taker call Midnight.take() with a valid offer where the Merkle proof is correct but the root in ratifierData is a different valid root (e.g., a root from a different tree that the maker also signed), causing isLeaf to return false (wrong root for this offer) and revert InvalidProof, blocking the take even though the maker signed both roots? Preconditions: maker has two separate Merkle trees with different roots; taker uses root from tree A with proof from tree B. Call sequence: taker calls Midnight.take(offer_from_A, ratifierData_with_root_B, ...); isRatified calls HashLib.isLeaf(root_B, hashOffer(offer_from_A), leafIndex, proof_from_B); computes wrong root; returns false; reverts InvalidProof. Invariant tested: isRatified must reject cross-tree proof mixing; each offer must use its own tree's root and proof. Scoped impact: fund freeze — taker cannot fill valid offer if they mix roots and proofs from different trees. Proof idea: unit test with cross-tree ratifierData and assert InvalidProof revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: cancelRoot] Can a taker (who is also an authorized operator of the maker) call cancelRoot(maker, root) to cancel the maker's offer tree, then immediately call Midnight.take() on a different offer from the same maker using a different ratifier, effectively using their authorization to DoS the maker's EcrecoverRatifier-based offers while still being able to take other offers? Preconditions: taker is authorized by maker; maker has offers using EcrecoverRatifier; taker calls cancelRoot then take with different ratifier. Call sequence: taker calls EcrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot(maker, root); taker calls Midnight.take(offer_with_different_ratifier, ...); first take succeeds; all EcrecoverRatifier offers are blocked. Invariant tested: authorization must not enable selective DoS of maker's offer set; coarse authorization must not allow operators to cancel roots. Scoped impact: fund freeze — maker's EcrecoverRatifier offers are permanently blocked; gate bypass via authorization abuse. Proof idea: unit test where authorized taker cancels root and assert subsequent EcrecoverRatifier takes revert RootCanceled.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: isRatified] Can a taker call Midnight.take() with a valid offer where the offer's ratifier field is set to address(ecrecoverRatifier) but the maker authorized a different ratifier address in Midnight.isAuthorized, causing Midnight.take to revert RatifierUnauthorized before even calling isRatified, blocking the take even though the offer is correctly signed? Preconditions: maker authorizes ratifier_v1 in Midnight; offer.ratifier = ratifier_v2 (different EcrecoverRatifier deployment); offer is signed for ratifier_v2's domain separator. Call sequence: taker calls Midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, ...); Midnight checks isAuthorized[offer.maker][offer.ratifier] == isAuthorized[maker][ratifier_v2] == false; reverts RatifierUnauthorized before calling isRatified. Invariant tested: maker must authorize the exact ratifier address used in the offer; ratifier address is part of the offer hash and authorization check. Scoped impact: fund freeze — taker cannot fill valid offer if maker authorized wrong ratifier address. Proof idea: unit test with mismatched ratifier authorization and assert RatifierUnauthorized revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: isRatified] Can a taker call Midnight.take() with a valid offer where the offer's maker field is set to a contract address that has no private key (e.g., a multisig or smart contract wallet), causing ecrecover to never recover the maker's address, and requiring the maker to authorize a delegate signer, but if that delegate's authorization is revoked, all offers are permanently blocked? Preconditions: maker is a smart contract (no private key); maker authorizes a delegate signer; delegate signs offers; maker revokes delegate. Call sequence: maker (contract) calls Midnight.setIsAuthorized(delegate, true, maker); delegate signs offers; maker calls Midnight.setIsAuthorized(delegate, false, maker); taker calls Midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, ...); isRatified calls ecrecover which never returns maker's address; checks isAuthorized(maker, _signer) == false; reverts Unauthorized. Invariant tested: smart contract makers must maintain delegate authorization; revocation permanently blocks all signed offers. Scoped impact: fund freeze — all offers signed by revoked delegate are permanently blocked for contract makers. Proof idea: unit test with contract maker and delegate revocation; assert Unauthorized revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: isRatified] Can a taker call Midnight.take() with a valid offer where the offer's market.maturity is exactly at block.timestamp (maturity boundary), causing the offer hash to include a specific maturity value, and if the taker's ratifierData was computed for a slightly different maturity (e.g., off by 1 second), causing isLeaf to return false and revert InvalidProof, blocking the take at the maturity boundary? Preconditions: maker signs offer with market.maturity = T; taker submits take at block.timestamp = T with ratifierData computed for maturity = T-1. Call sequence: taker calls Midnight.take(offer_with_maturity_T, ratifierData_for_maturity_T_minus_1, ...); isRatified calls HashLib.hashOffer(offer_with_maturity_T) which hashes differently; isLeaf returns false; reverts InvalidProof. Invariant tested: offer hash must exactly match the signed offer including maturity; maturity boundary must not cause hash mismatches. Scoped impact: fund freeze — taker cannot fill valid offer if maturity in offer differs from ratifierData. Proof idea: unit test with maturity off by 1 and assert InvalidProof revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: isRatified] Can a taker call Midnight.take() with a valid offer where the offer's group field (bytes32) is set to a value that has been consumed to type(uint256).max via Midnight.setConsumed, causing the take to revert ConsumedUnits or ConsumedAssets in Midnight before isRatified is even called, blocking the take even though the offer is correctly ratified? Preconditions: maker has a valid signed offer with group G; an authorized operator calls Midnight.setConsumed(G, type(uint256).max, maker); taker attempts to fill the offer. Call sequence: operator calls Midnight.setConsumed(G, type(uint256).max, maker); taker calls Midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, ...); Midnight increments consumed[maker][G] which overflows or exceeds maxUnits; reverts ConsumedUnits. Invariant tested: setConsumed with type(uint256).max must permanently cancel all offers in the group; this is documented behavior but must not be bypassable. Scoped impact: fund freeze — all offers in the group are permanently blocked via setConsumed. Proof idea: unit test where operator sets consumed to max and assert subsequent takes revert ConsumedUnits.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol'] [Function: isRatified] Can a taker call Midnight.take() with a valid offer where the offer's tick is not accessible (tick % tickSpacing != 0), causing Midnight.take to revert TickNotAccessible before calling isRatified, blocking the take even though the offer is correctly signed and ratified? Preconditions: maker signs offer with tick = 100; market tickSpacing is later reduced to 3 (100 % 3 != 0); taker attempts to fill the offer. Call sequence: tickSpacingSetter calls Midnight.setMarketTickSpacing(id, 3); taker calls Midnight.take(offer_with_tick_100, ratifierData, ...); Midnight checks offer.tick % _marketState.tickSpacing == 0 which fails; reverts TickNotAccessible before calling isRatified. Invariant tested: tick spacing reduction must not retroactively invalidate existing signed offers; valid offers must remain fillable after tick spacing changes. Scoped impact: fund freeze — all offers with ticks not divisible by new spacing are permanently blocked after tick spacing reduction. Proof idea: unit test with tick spacing reduction and assert TickNotAccessible revert for previously valid offers.
